title: Why You Feel Safer Helping Others With Money Than Helping Yourself
body:
Why You Feel Safer Helping Others With Money Than Helping Yourself

You're scrolling through your feed. A friend posts about a crowdfunding campaign for a medical emergency. Maybe it's a GoFundMe for a struggling artist, or a direct plea from a family member who's just lost their job. Without a second thought, you click. You donate. You share. You feel a surge of warmth, a sense of purpose. You're helping. You're good. But then, later that same day, you open your own banking app. You see your own numbers. The same numbers that feel perpetually "not enough." The same numbers that trigger a familiar knot of anxiety in your stomach. And the thought of investing in that course you've been eyeing, or hiring that coach, or even just buying yourself something nice that isn't strictly "necessary"? It feels like pulling teeth. It feels selfish. It feels dangerous. Know what I mean?

This isn't just about charity versus self-indulgence. This is about a deep-seated, often unconscious pattern that keeps you stuck. It's about why helping others with money feels safe, even noble, while helping yourself with money feels fraught with peril. Truth is, you're not alone in this. It's a common, heartbreaking paradox I see all the time. You're a giver, a caretaker, a fixer. And somewhere along the line, you learned that your value is tied to what you provide for others, not what you provide for yourself.

The Echo Chamber of "Not Enough"

Let's get real. Your financial reality today isn't just about your income or your expenses. It's a living, breathing story. And usually, it's a story you didn't even write. It's the story of your parents' struggles, their anxieties, their beliefs about money. It's the story of societal messages that glorify self-sacrifice and demonize self-interest. It's the story of a culture that tells you there's always more to do, more to achieve, more to give, before you can finally rest or receive. You feel that?

Think about it. How many times have you heard or internalized phrases like, "Money doesn't grow on trees," "We can't afford that," or "You need to save for a rainy day"? These aren't inherently bad statements. But when they're delivered with a pervasive undercurrent of fear, scarcity, and shame, they embed themselves deep within your subconscious. They become your operating system for money. And when you see someone else in need, that ingrained belief system kicks in. "I have to help. If I don't, I'm bad. If I don't, I'm selfish."

Bari Tessler, author of The Art of Money, talks extensively about how our money stories are formed in childhood. She emphasizes that understanding these narratives is the first step toward rewriting them. You're not just dealing with present-day finances. You're carrying generations of financial trauma and belief systems. This isn't your fault, but it is your responsibility to unpack. It's why you can feel so generous with others, yet so tight-fisted with yourself. You're playing out an old script where your needs come last. Always.

And then there's the societal conditioning that screams, "Self-care is selfish!" We see it everywhere. The pressure to always be "on," always producing, always giving. To prioritize your own well-being, especially financially, can feel like a direct rebellion against everything you've been taught. But here's the thing. You can't pour from an empty cup. You've heard it a million times, but do you actually believe it when it comes to your money? Or do you believe you need to keep pouring, even if your cup is bone dry, because someone else "needs it more"?

The Illusion of Control: Why Giving Feels Safe

When you help someone else with money, there's a clear transaction. You send the money. They receive it. You see the immediate impact. There's a tangible outcome. This creates a powerful, albeit often false, sense of control. You've "solved" a problem, or at least alleviated some suffering. This feeling of agency is incredibly comforting, especially when your own financial situation feels chaotic or overwhelming.

Conversely, investing in yourself. that's a different beast. It's an act of faith. You're spending money on something that might not have an immediate, measurable return. You're betting on yourself. And for many, that feels incredibly risky. Why? Because you've been conditioned to believe that you're not a safe bet. You've been taught that external validation is more reliable than internal worth. So, when you give to others, you get that external validation. You're a hero. When you give to yourself, you're just... you. And for many, that's not enough.

Kate Northrup, in Money: A Love Story, challenges us to see money as a form of energy. When you give money to someone else, you're sending that energy outward. It feels expansive. It feels good. But when you receive money, or allow yourself to spend it on your own growth and well-being, that energy has to flow inward. And for many of us, receiving feels far more vulnerable than giving. It requires you to open up, to be seen, to acknowledge your own needs. It requires you to believe you're worthy of that energy.

This isn't about being selfish. It's about recognizing that your capacity to give effectively to others is directly tied to how well you've resourced yourself. If you're constantly depleting your own financial reserves out of a sense of obligation or guilt, you're not truly helping. You're just perpetuating a cycle of scarcity, both for yourself and, energetically, for those you're trying to help. Wealth isn't what you accumulate. It's what you stop fearing.

The Trauma Response of Financial Self-Sabotage

It sounds dramatic, I know. But often, your inability to financially support yourself stems from deeper, unresolved trauma. This isn't always capital "T" Trauma. It can be the small, insidious traumas of growing up in scarcity, or witnessing constant financial stress, or being shamed for wanting things. Geneen Roth, a pioneer in emotional eating and money work, often speaks about how our relationship with money mirrors our relationship with food. Both are about nourishment, safety, and control. And both can become battlegrounds where old wounds play out.

When you encounter a situation where you need to spend money on yourself - whether it's for therapy, a business investment, or simply a much-needed vacation - your nervous system can go into overdrive. It's not just a financial decision. It's a survival response. Your body remembers the fear, the scarcity, the shame associated with money from your past. So, instead of moving forward, you freeze. Or you fight - with yourself. Or you flee - from the opportunity. This is why you can feel so paralyzed when it comes to your own financial growth, yet so decisive when helping a friend.

In his work, Gabor Maté explains that trauma isn't just a story from the past. It's how the past lives in your body, impacting your present behavior. Your financial patterns are often a manifestation of this. When you struggle to invest in yourself, it's not because you're inherently flawed or lazy. It's because your nervous system is trying to protect you from perceived danger. The perceived danger of "not enough," of failure, of being judged. And the easiest way to avoid that perceived danger? Keep giving to others. Keep yourself small. Keep yourself "safe."

Understanding this connection between your past experiences and your current money patterns is crucial. Books like It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn can help you explore how inherited family trauma impacts your life, including your finances. And Bessel van der Kolk's The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) illuminates how trauma physically manifests and how to begin healing it. Your bank account is a mirror, not a scorecard. It reflects these deeper patterns.

Reclaiming Your Financial Autonomy: A Path to Self-Compassion

So, how do you break this cycle? How do you move from feeling safer helping others to feeling safe helping yourself? It starts with radical self-compassion. It starts with recognizing that you are worthy of receiving, just as you are worthy of giving. It starts with challenging those deeply ingrained beliefs that tell you your needs are secondary.

First, you need to become intimately aware of your money story. Journal about your earliest memories of money. What did your parents say about it? How did they act? What were the unspoken rules? Identify the core beliefs that are driving your current behavior. Don't judge them. Just observe them. Like Alan Watts said, "Trying to manage things, trying to force things, just creates more problems." You're just watching the story unfold, for now.

Second, start small. If the idea of a huge investment in yourself feels terrifying, begin with micro-investments. Can you buy that book you've been wanting? Can you sign up for a single yoga class? Can you treat yourself to a really good coffee without guilt? These small acts of self-nurturing, when done consciously and without shame, begin to rewire your brain. They teach your nervous system that it's safe to receive. They teach you that you are worthy of good things.

Third, practice conscious receiving. This isn't just about money. It's about allowing yourself to receive compliments, help, love, and support without feeling the need to immediately reciprocate or diminish it. This builds your capacity to hold good things, including financial abundance, for yourself. When someone offers you help, say "thank you" instead of "oh, you don't have to." It's a small shift, but it's powerful.

Fourth, set boundaries. This is crucial, especially if you're a natural giver. It's okay to say no to financial requests that stretch you too thin. It's okay to prioritize your own financial stability and growth. This isn't selfish. It's responsible. It's a necessary step toward creating true abundance, not just for yourself, but for your ability to genuinely help others from a place of overflow, not depletion.

Beyond Guilt: Embracing a New Financial Identity

This journey isn't about eradicating your desire to help others. That's a beautiful part of who you are. It's about shifting the foundation of that giving. It's about moving from giving out of guilt, obligation, or a desperate need for external validation, to giving from a place of genuine abundance and choice. When you're financially resourced, when you've invested in your own growth and well-being, your giving becomes more powerful, more sustainable, and ultimately, more impactful.

Imagine a life where you feel as comfortable investing in your own future as you do contributing to a friend's fundraiser. A life where you see your own needs as valid and important. A life where money is just energy with a story attached to it, and you get to be the author of that story. Not your parents, not society, not your past traumas. You. Does that land?

This isn't an overnight fix. It's a process of unlearning and relearning. It requires patience, self-compassion, and a willingness to sit with discomfort. But the payoff is immense: true financial freedom, not just in your bank account, but in your heart and soul. It's about becoming a fully resourced, fully self-expressed human being who can give freely because you've first given to yourself. As Tara Brach teaches in Radical Acceptance (paid link), this self-compassion is the path to liberation.

What would it feel like to trust yourself with money? To truly believe you are worthy of your own investment? To know that by nurturing yourself, you're not taking away from others, but actually expanding your capacity to contribute to the world in meaningful ways? This is the work. This is the healing. And it begins with you, right now.

FAQ

Q: Isn't it just irresponsible to spend money on myself when others are struggling?

A: This is a common and understandable fear, but it's often rooted in a scarcity mindset. If you're constantly depleting yourself to help others, you're not truly sustainable. Think of it like putting on your own oxygen mask first on an airplane. You can't effectively help others if you're suffocating. Investing in your own well-being, growth, and financial stability allows you to help from a place of strength and abundance, rather than guilt or obligation. It's about creating a sustainable model for giving.

Q: How do I distinguish between genuine need and my own guilt when someone asks for money?

A: This requires self-awareness and boundaries. First, check in with yourself. What's your immediate emotional response? Is it a genuine desire to help, or a feeling of pressure, obligation, or fear of judgment? Second, consider your own financial situation. Is this request truly within your means without causing undue stress or compromising your own financial goals? It's okay to say no, or to offer help in non-monetary ways if that feels more aligned. Setting clear boundaries isn't selfish; it's a sign of self-respect and financial maturity.

Q: What if I feel guilty about spending money on "non-essentials" for myself?

A: Many of us are conditioned to believe that money should only be spent on necessities. But what constitutes a "necessity" can be subjective. Your mental health, your personal growth, your joy, and your rest are all essential components of a well-lived life. If spending money on a hobby, a relaxing experience, or a course that feeds your soul genuinely nourishes you and contributes to your overall well-being, then it's not a frivolous expense. It's an investment in yourself. Start by reframing these expenses as investments in your human capital, your joy, and your capacity to thrive.

Q: How can I start to shift my mindset around receiving money for myself?

A: Start with small, conscious acts of receiving. When someone offers you a compliment, simply say "thank you" instead of deflecting. If a friend offers to treat you to coffee, accept it graciously. Practice visualizing yourself receiving abundance - not just money, but opportunities, support, and love. Affirm to yourself daily that you are worthy of receiving good things. Remember, money is just energy with a story attached to it. You're rewriting that story to include your own worthiness to receive.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover why helping others financially feels safer than helping yourself. Explore the psychology behind this common behavior and gain insight into your own giv
author: Kalesh
